topic: Someone discovers their recipe has become famous under another name
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the glossy magazine spread. The headline screamed: "The Alchemist's Secret: A Symphony of Spices." Her hands were shaking so badly she had to grip the counter edge of the kitchen island. She had poured her heart, and perhaps a little too much of her soul, into that simple lentil soup recipe. It was just her grandmother's old book, a comfort on a cold Tuesday. Now, it was a global phenomenon, rebranded, repackaged, and sold for exorbitant sums under the moniker "The Golden Broth." A cold knot tightened in her stomach. She slowly reached for the cookbook, her fingers trembling as she flipped to the introduction. The words spoke of a culinary titan, someone who clearly didn't know where their inspiration truly began. A sharp, involuntary gasp escaped her lips. She quickly slammed the book shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet apartment.

[story 2]

"This isn't real," Marcus muttered, wiping a bead of sweat from his forehead with the back of his wrist. He kept glancing at the phone screen, where a notification popped up—a trending topic alert. His grandmother’s recipe for sourdough starter, a humble collection of flour, water, and patience, was now the centerpiece of a major food blog. He walked to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to see the bustling street below. People were stopping, taking photos of loaves that bore his grandmother's humble markings, attributing the magic to some anonymous, celebrated chef. A wave of nausea washed over him. He felt his breath catch in his throat, shallow and quick. He needed to check the comments section, to see if anyone had noticed the subtle, almost accidental detail in the instructions—the specific way he always measured the yeast.

[story 3]

I found the mention tucked away in an obscure online forum, buried beneath discussions about obscure fermentation techniques. "The Nebula Noodle." My hands began to sweat, slicking the keyboard beneath my fingertips. It was my recipe for quick-pickled onions, a simple, almost throwaway dish I used to make for late-night snacks. Now, it was being hailed as revolutionary, a culinary masterpiece attributed to a phantom chef. Every time I reread the description, a strange pressure built behind my eyes. It felt like the air in the room was growing thick, suffocating. I stood up abruptly, knocking over a stack of cookbooks. The sound was jarring, and I pressed my palms hard against my temples, trying to anchor myself to something solid as the reality of the situation seemed to tilt and spin.
